item.title: bulb
item.description: A layered underground storage organ that contains a compressed stem surrounded by fleshy scale leaves filled with stored nutrients. A bulb produces a new plant each growing season from its center. In the garden, bulbs are planted at a specified depth and orientation (pointed end up). Common garden bulbs include garlic and onions.

item.title: compost
item.description: Decomposed organic material (plant scraps, leaves, food waste) used as a soil amendment. Compost improves soil structure, adds nutrients, increases water-holding capacity, and supports beneficial soil organisms. It is produced by piling organic material and allowing microorganisms to break it down over weeks to months. Finished compost is dark, crumbly, and earthy-smelling.

item.title: cutting
item.description: A piece of stem, leaf, or root removed from a parent plant and placed in water or a growing medium until it develops its own roots. The resulting plant is genetically identical to the parent. Cuttings are used when an exact copy of a plant is wanted, when a plant does not produce viable seeds easily, or when a gardener wants to multiply a plant they already have. Common plants started from cuttings include rosemary, basil, and mint.

item.title: division
item.description: The process of splitting a mature plant’s root system into two or more separate plants, each with a portion of roots and stems or buds. Division works with plants that naturally form clumps or spreading root systems. It is used to propagate perennials, to spread a plant to new locations, or to rejuvenate an overcrowded clump whose growth has declined. Common plants propagated by division include chives, rhubarb, and strawberries.

item.title: fertilizer
item.description: A material added to soil or applied to plants to supply nutrients — primarily nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K). Fertilizers are labeled with three numbers representing the percentage of each (e.g., 10-10-10). Organic fertilizers (bone meal, blood meal, fish emulsion) release nutrients slowly as they decompose. Synthetic fertilizers release nutrients immediately but do not improve soil structure. Fertilizer is applied when soil nutrients are insufficient for the plant being grown, as determined by a soil test or by visible nutrient deficiency symptoms.

item.title: frost date
item.description: The average date on which the last spring frost or first autumn frost occurs in a given location. The last frost date marks when it is generally safe to plant frost-sensitive crops outdoors. The first frost date marks when the growing season for those crops ends. The interval between the two is the frost-free growing season. Frost dates are averages based on historical weather data and are published by cooperative extension services and regional gardening organizations.

item.title: frost pocket
item.description: A low-lying area where cold air collects on still nights. Because cold air is denser than warm air, it flows downhill and pools in depressions, valley floors, and areas blocked by walls or dense hedges. Frost pockets experience frost earlier in autumn and later in spring than surrounding higher ground, shortening the effective growing season for that spot.

item.title: hardening off
item.description: The process of gradually exposing indoor-grown seedlings to outdoor conditions over the course of a week before transplanting them permanently. Seedlings are placed outside for increasing periods each day, allowing them to adjust to direct sunlight, wind, and temperature fluctuations. Skipping this step often results in transplant shock.

item.title: mulch
item.description: A layer of material spread on the soil surface around plants. Mulch reduces water evaporation, moderates soil temperature, suppresses weed growth, and (if organic) adds nutrients as it decomposes. Common mulch materials include wood chips, straw, shredded leaves, and compost. Inorganic mulches such as gravel or landscape fabric suppress weeds but do not add nutrients.

item.title: raised bed
item.description: A contained growing area where the soil surface is elevated above the surrounding ground level, typically by building a frame from wood, stone, or metal and filling it with soil and compost. Raised beds provide control over soil quality and drainage, warm up faster in spring, and reduce bending during garden work. They are used when the native soil is poor, compacted, contaminated, or poorly drained. Common dimensions are 1.0–1.2 m (3–4 ft) wide (for reach from both sides) and at least 15–30 cm (6–12 in) deep.

item.title: rhizome
item.description: A horizontal underground stem that grows outward from the parent plant, producing new shoots and roots along its length. Rhizomes can be divided into sections and replanted to propagate the plant. Examples of rhizome-propagated plants include ginger and iris.

item.title: seed
item.description: A dormant plant embryo enclosed in a protective coat, along with a small supply of stored food. Given the right conditions — moisture, warmth, and sometimes light or a period of cold — the seed germinates: the embryo breaks through the coat, sends a root downward, and pushes a shoot upward. Seeds are the product of sexual reproduction and carry genetic material from both parent plants.

item.title: seed packet
item.description: The packaging in which seeds are sold, containing both the seeds and printed growing information. A seed packet typically lists: the plant species and cultivar name, days to maturity, light requirements, spacing and planting depth, when to sow (relative to frost dates), and a brief description of the plant’s characteristics. Seed catalogs provide the same information in a browsable format, often with more detail on cultivar selection, disease resistance, and regional suitability.

item.title: tuber
item.description: A thickened portion of an underground stem that stores energy for the plant. Each “eye” (growth bud) on a tuber can produce a new shoot. In gardening, tubers are planted directly in the ground at the appropriate depth and spacing. The most common garden tuber is the potato.

item.title: annual
item.description: A plant that completes its entire life cycle — germination, growth, flowering, seed production, and death — in a single growing season. Most common garden vegetables (tomatoes, basil, lettuce, beans) are annuals.

item.title: biennial
item.description: A plant that takes two growing seasons to complete its life cycle. In the first year it grows leaves and roots; in the second year it flowers, produces seed, and dies. Examples include carrots, parsley, and onions.

item.title: cooperative extension
item.description: A university-affiliated program that translates agricultural and horticultural research into practical guidance for the public. Cooperative extension services exist in every U.S. state and many other countries under different names. They publish regionally specific growing guides, perform soil testing, and provide advice based on local conditions. They are among the most reliable sources of gardening information for a specific area.

item.title: cultivar
item.description: A specific version of a plant species that has been selected or bred for particular traits, such as fruit size, color, disease resistance, or climate tolerance. Short for “cultivated variety.” Two cultivars of the same species may have different temperature tolerances, days to maturity, or disease resistance.

item.title: days to maturity
item.description: The number of days from planting (for direct-sown crops) or transplanting (for transplanted crops) until the plant is ready to harvest. Listed on seed packets and in catalogs, this number is compared to the length of the local frost-free growing season to determine whether a crop can be grown outdoors in a given location.

item.title: direct sowing
item.description: Planting seeds directly into the soil where the plant will grow to maturity, without starting them indoors or in a separate container first. Direct sowing is the simplest propagation method and works well when the plant tolerates local conditions at planting time and the growing season is long enough for it to reach maturity from an outdoor start.

item.title: drainage
item.description: How quickly water moves through soil. Drainage depends on soil texture and structure. Well-drained soil allows water to pass through within a few hours; poorly drained soil holds water for a day or more, which can suffocate roots. Drainage is tested by filling a 30 cm deep hole with water and timing how long it takes to empty.

item.title: frost
item.description: A weather condition that occurs when the air temperature at ground level drops to 0°C (32°F) or below. Water inside plant cells freezes, expands, and ruptures cell walls, causing damage that is often fatal to tender plants. The average dates of last spring frost and first autumn frost define the boundaries of a location’s growing season.

item.title: growing method
item.description: The approach used to cultivate a plant, encompassing where it is grown (in-ground, raised bed, container, indoors), how it is propagated (seed, transplant, cutting, division), and any modifications to growing conditions (soil amendment, irrigation, season extension). The choice of growing method follows from comparing a plant’s requirements to a site’s conditions.

item.title: hardiness zone
item.description: A geographic classification based on the average annual minimum winter temperature. The USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map divides North America into zones numbered 1 through 13, each subdivided into “a” and “b” halves. A plant’s hardiness zone rating indicates the coldest zone in which it can survive winter.

item.title: loam
item.description: A soil texture that is a roughly equal blend of sand, silt, and clay. Loam is generally considered ideal for gardening because it balances drainage, water retention, and nutrient availability. It holds shape loosely when squeezed but crumbles when poked.

item.title: microclimate
item.description: The climate conditions of a small, specific area that differ from the surrounding region. In gardening, microclimates are created by factors such as proximity to buildings, pavement, bodies of water, elevation changes, and wind exposure. A south-facing wall, for example, creates a warmer microclimate than an open north-facing slope at the same location.

item.title: organic matter
item.description: Decomposed plant and animal material in soil. Organic matter holds water like a sponge, releases nutrients as it decomposes further, and binds mineral particles into aggregates that improve soil structure. Gardeners increase organic matter by adding compost, aged manure, or leaf mold.

item.title: perennial
item.description: A plant that lives for more than two years. Many perennials die back to the ground in winter and regrow from their roots in spring. Examples include rhubarb, asparagus, and most fruit trees.

item.title: photosynthesis
item.description: The process by which plants convert light energy, water, and carbon dioxide into sugars and oxygen. It occurs primarily in the leaves and is the foundation of all plant growth. Without adequate light for photosynthesis, a plant cannot produce enough energy to grow, flower, or fruit.

item.title: soil amendment
item.description: Any material added to soil to change its properties. Common amendments include compost (adds organic matter and nutrients), lime (raises pH), sulfur (lowers pH), sand (improves drainage in clay), and peat moss or coconut coir (improves water retention in sandy soil). Amendments are chosen based on the gap between a soil’s current condition and what the intended plant needs.

item.title: soil pH
item.description: A measure of how acidic or alkaline soil is, on a scale from 0 to 14. A pH of 7 is neutral; below 7 is acidic; above 7 is alkaline. Soil pH controls which nutrients are available to plant roots. Most garden plants grow best in slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6.0–7.0). Soil pH is tested with an inexpensive home kit or through a cooperative extension service.

item.title: transplanting
item.description: The act of moving a plant from one growing location to another, typically from an indoor container to an outdoor garden bed. Transplanting is stressful for plants because roots are disturbed and environmental conditions change abruptly. Hardening off (gradually exposing seedlings to outdoor conditions over a week) reduces transplant shock.
